need to complete migration well before October to allow a buffer period without SalesforceChristina, as new Director of Operations, will manage day-to-day activities and coordinate cross-departmental inputThe goal is to avoid "garbage in, garbage out" by careful planning of data migration and implementation sequenceCameron highlighted typical steps like configuring contact and company objects and custom properties with attention to multifamily industry needsStrategic Discovery and Process Documentation will drive the migration approach and HubSpot setup (11:10)Jeremy and Christina are to simplify quoting and contract processes (27:00)Jeremy clarified that quote signatures are tied to sales quotes, not separate ad hoc signaturesCameron suggested exploring HubSpot’s Commerce Hub for QuotaPath integrated with HubSpot and QuickBooks (30:39)Jeremy wants transparent commission data linked to actual customer payments to reduce repetitive queriesCameron confirmed QuotaPath can automate commission calculations based on sales data and payment statusBilly highlighted the ease of updating commissions regularly and customizing structures per repIntegration with External Databases like Elastic and ALN for multifamily data automation and reporting (21:00)Jeremy asked about syncing HubSpot with Elastic; Billy explained data replication tools like AirByte or Fivetran enable UsabilityThe team emphasized balancing rich data capture with ease of use for sales to ensure adoption and data quality.Simplifying Sales Data Entry While Preserving Data Depth is a key goal for the HubSpot implementation (17:00)Christina’s experience showed Salesforce was overloaded and complicated for sales, leading to data quality issuesShe seeks dynamic fields and dependencies to auto-fill data and reduce manual entry burden without losing critical infoBilly confirmed the system design will maintain granular data behind a user-friendly interfaceData Accuracy and Reporting Foundation built on clean, linked data to support workflows and analytics (18:30)Proper data tagging and indexing enable AI and workflow automation to function effectivelyMaintaining data integrity avoids junk results and supports visualization and mermaid diagrams of process flowsThe team discussed replicating spreadsheet-style views with database efficiency to combine usability with performanceMitigating Data Duplication Risks During Migration through use of unique customer identifiers and careful data mapping (24:00)Christina prefers a master spreadsheet approach with a customer number to connect records during uploadsBilly explained databases handle granular data without repetition, improving performance and traceabilityThe system will keep change history, enabling drill-down and filtered views for auditabilityStrategic Partnership and Project ManagementThe consulting team will serve as a thought partner, helping define roles, responsibilities, and cost models while supporting implementation.Collaborative Role Definition and Cost Discussions to clarify what the client team can do and where consultants add value (06:00)Jeremy expressed desire to pay fairly for migration work but remain cost-conscious on advisory servicesBilly proposed helping define strategy and responsibilities so client can choose self-service or consultant-led optionsThe team agreed the consultants emphasized breaking down the project into discrete initiatives to deliver value early and avoid overwhelmCameron recommended aligning with sales and operations stakeholders for focused discovery CRM Transition Plan: Team aims to migrate from Salesforce to HubSpot before October contract end, ensuring minimal disruption.Best Practices Emphasis: Focus on refining current processes to match HubSpot’s capabilities, not just replicating old workflows.Tech Stack Consolidation: Investigating HubSpot’s capacity to integrate functions from multiple tools like ClickUp and Calendly.Custom Objects for Real Estate: Evaluating the need for custom data structures in HubSpot for multifamily real estate tracking.Consultant Relationship: Desire for clear, cost-efficient partnerships with consultants who provide strategic advice without hidden fees.Phased Implementation Strategy: Gradual rollout of features to minimize risk, aligning with thorough documentation and cross-department coordination.CRM Migration and Implementation PlanningThe team is focused on transitioning from Salesforce to HubSpot CRM smoothly before the Salesforce contract ends in October.Urgent Need to Start Data Migration and CRM Setup (05:04)Jeremy emphasized completing migration well before the Salesforce contract ends in October.Christina, the new Director of Operations, will lead day-to-day management of the transition.They want clarity on what tasks to outsource versus what can be handled internally to optimize costs.The goal is a clean data transfer with minimal user disruption and no garbage data entry.Defining Best Practices for HubSpot Implementation (09:25)The team wants to move beyond simply replicating old Salesforce processes.They aim to identify ideal workflows that fit HubSpot’s tools and capabilities.Billy Leigh stressed the importance of understanding current processes and refining them for HubSpot.This involves documenting workflows and building a roadmap for transition.Consolidation of Tech Stack to Improve Workflow (09:45)Current systems include Salesforce, ClickUp, Calendly, and DocuSign, which create workflow complexity.Jeremy and Billy want to explore how much of these functions HubSpot can absorb to reduce tool sprawl.Integration or elimination of redundant tools is part of the planning to improve user experience.This will streamline operations and reduce switching between platforms.Collaborative Roadmap Development and Discovery (13:17)Cameron outlined plans to regroup with the internal team lead after his return to dive deeper into Salesforce migration details.The implementation will focus on custom objects, properties, and relationships relevant to the multifamily real estate sector.Discovery will clarify what HubSpot native capabilities to use versus external tools for CPQ and other needs.This phased approach balances quick wins with long-term infrastructure planning.Industry Focus and Customization NeedsThe company’s strong presence in the multifamily real estate sector shapes CRM customization and data management priorities.Heavy Multi-Family Real Estate Orientation (14:19)Jeremy confirmed their core business is multifamily real estate with some senior living exposure.This sector requires specific tracking and relationship management features in the CRM.Cameron and Billy highlighted their experience working with multifamily private equity clients in Dallas, adding relevant expertise.Strong industry alignment will help tailor HubSpot configurations effectively.Custom Object and Data Structure Considerations (16:32)Discussion focused on whether custom objects for properties are needed in HubSpot or if simpler setups suffice.Christina will lead translating current Salesforce data structures into HubSpot while balancing sales team ease of use.The goal is to maintain necessary data enrichment without overcomplicating user input requirements.This balance supports both operational needs and front-line sales adoption.Leveraging Internal Expertise for Implementation (16:46)Christina’s prior experience with similar data structures at Comax is seen as a major asset.Her role will be critical to simplifying complex data for practical sales use while preserving backend richness.This internal leadership helps align data migration with real-world sales processes and goals.It also reduces reliance on external consultants for detailed system design.Consulting Partnership and Cost ManagementThe company wants a partnership approach with consultants focused on practical advice and cost efficiency.Clear Roles and Cost Transparency Desired (05:25)Jeremy expressed desire to define clear engagement rules with consultants to avoid surprises.They want to know approximate costs upfront for major work like data migration.The team prefers consultants to act as partners offering strategic advice, not just selling additional services.Jeremy values suggestions but will decide based on cost-effectiveness.Flexible Support Model from Consultants (06:32)Billy Leigh outlined a flexible approach where consultants help with strategy and execution as needed.They can advise on what tasks clients can handle internally versus what requires third-party help.This model supports the company’s goal of minimizing external spend while still accessing expertise.Consultants will focus on delivering value and building a roadmap rather than pushing unnecessary solutions.Open to HubSpot Native and External Tool Suggestions (07:15)The company is open to exploring HubSpot’s native CPQ and other tools.They want unbiased advice on whether to use native features, third-party apps, or maintain current tools.Consultants will provide options with pros and cons to help the team choose cost-effective, scalable solutions.This approach ensures decisions are aligned with business needs, not sales pitches.Operational Readiness and Documentation EffortsThe team is actively documenting current processes to prepare for the migration and future state design.Rapid Onboarding of New Director of Operations (10:56)Christina started just days before the meeting and is already leading documentation efforts.They are capturing existing workflows and data management practices in detail.This documentation forms the foundation for migration planning and process redesign.Early involvement ensures operational realities shape technology choices.Focus on Data Quality and Access Control (08:30)They are limiting current Salesforce user access to prevent data corruption during transition.Emphasis is on “garbage in, garbage out” to avoid importing flawed data into HubSpot.Ensuring clean data supports reliable reporting and user confidence post-migration.This step is critical for a successful CRM go-live.Phased Implementation with Cross-Department Coordination (10:10)Christina will coordinate with various teams to cover all use cases and data needs.They plan to roll out features and data migration in stages to reduce risk.This phased approach allows for adjustment and feedback before full switch-over.The plan balances speed with thoroughness to meet the October deadline.Data-Driven Approach to Migration and Implementation (11:37)Jeremy emphasized their culture’s reliance on data to guide decisions.They want measurable success criteria for each phase of the transition.Consultants will help design metrics and dashboards to monitor progress and quality.This ensures accountability and continuous improvement throughout implementation.Action ItemsJeremy WileyCoordinate internally to continue documenting current Salesforce and related processes as a basis for HubSpot implementation. (10:56)Christina EdwardsLead detailed discovery and documentation of current processes across departments to define HubSpot implementation needs. (02:22)Cameron TaggartReview and extract relevant details from a prior call with Ryan pertaining to Salesforce migration and HubSpot implementation requirements. (13:00)Coordinate follow-up session with HelloSpoke leadership including Kyle and Ryan for deeper discovery on Salesforce migration and HubSpot configuration planning once Kyle returns. (13:00)Billy LeighContinue to serve as thought partner for defining roles, responsibilities, and best practice approaches balancing consulting and client-controlled work. (05:25)

garbage data entry.Defining Best Practices for HubSpot Implementation (09:25)The team wants to move beyond simply replicating old Salesforce processes.They aim to identify ideal workflows that fit HubSpot’s tools and capabilities.Billy Leigh stressed the importance of understanding current processes and refining them for HubSpot.This involves documenting workflows and building a roadmap for transition.Consolidation of Tech Stack to Improve Workflow (09:45)Current systems include Salesforce, ClickUp, Calendly, and DocuSign, which create workflow complexity.Jeremy and Billy want to explore how much of these functions HubSpot can absorb to reduce tool sprawl.Integration or elimination of redundant tools is part of the planning to improve user experience.This will streamline operations and reduce switching between platforms.Collaborative Roadmap Development and Discovery (13:17)Cameron outlined plans to regroup with the internal team lead after his return to dive deeper into Salesforce migration details.The implementation will focus on custom objects, properties, and relationships relevant to the multifamily real estate sector.Discovery will clarify what HubSpot native capabilities to use versus external tools for CPQ and other needs.This phased approach balances quick wins with long-term infrastructure planning.Industry Focus and Customization NeedsThe company’s strong presence in the multifamily real estate sector shapes CRM customization and data management priorities.Heavy Multi-Family Real Estate Orientation (14:19)Jeremy confirmed their core business is multifamily real estate with some senior living exposure.This sector requires specific tracking and relationship management features in the CRM.Cameron and Billy highlighted their experience working with multifamily private equity clients in Dallas, adding relevant expertise.Strong industry alignment will help tailor HubSpot configurations effectively.Custom Object and Data Structure Considerations (16:32)Discussion focused on whether custom objects for properties are needed in HubSpot or if simpler setups suffice.Christina will lead translating current Salesforce data structures into HubSpot while balancing sales team ease of use.The goal is to maintain necessary data enrichment without overcomplicating user input requirements.This balance supports both operational needs and front-line sales adoption.Leveraging Internal Expertise for Implementation (16:46)Christina’s prior experience with similar data structures at Comax is seen as a major asset.Her role will be critical to simplifying complex data for practical sales use while preserving backend richness.This internal leadership helps align data migration with real-world sales processes and goals.It also reduces reliance on external consultants for detailed system design.Consulting Partnership and Cost ManagementThe company wants a partnership approach with consultants focused on practical advice and cost efficiency.Clear Roles and Cost Transparency Desired (05:25)Jeremy expressed desire to define clear engagement rules with consultants to avoid surprises.They want to know approximate costs upfront for major work like data migration.The team prefers consultants to act as partners offering strategic advice, not just selling additional services.Jeremy values suggestions but will decide based on cost-effectiveness.Flexible Support Model from Consultants (06:32)Billy Leigh outlined a flexible approach where consultants help with strategy and execution as needed.They can advise on what tasks clients can handle internally versus what requires third-party help.This model supports the company’s goal of minimizing external spend while still accessing expertise.Consultants will focus on delivering value and building a roadmap rather than pushing unnecessary solutions.Open to HubSpot Native and External Tool Suggestions (07:15)The company is open to exploring HubSpot’s native CPQ and other tools.They want unbiased advice on whether to use native features, third-party apps, or maintain current tools.Consultants will provide options with pros and cons to help the team choose cost-effective, scalable solutions.This approach ensures decisions are aligned with business needs, not sales pitches.Operational Readiness and Documentation EffortsThe team is actively documenting current processes to prepare for the migration and future state design.Rapid Onboarding of New Director of Operations (10:56)Christina started just days before the meeting and is already leading documentation efforts.They are capturing existing workflows and data management practices in detail.This documentation forms the foundation for migration planning and process redesign.Early involvement ensures operational realities shape technology choices.Focus on Data Quality and Access Control (08:30)They are limiting current Salesforce user access to prevent data corruption during transition.Emphasis is on “garbage in, garbage out” to avoid importing flawed data into HubSpot.Ensuring clean data supports reliable reporting and user confidence post-migration.This step is critical for a successful CRM go-live.Phased Implementation with Cross-Department Coordination (10:10)Christina will coordinate with various teams to cover all use cases and data needs.They plan to roll out features and data migration in stages to reduce risk.This phased approach allows for adjustment and feedback before full switch-over.The plan balances speed with thoroughness to meet the October deadline.Data-Driven Approach to Migration and Implementation (11:37)Jeremy emphasized their culture’s reliance on data to guide decisions.They want measurable success criteria for each phase of the transition.Consultants will help design metrics and dashboards to monitor progress and quality.This ensures accountability and continuous improvement throughout implementation.Action ItemsJeremy Current Commission Process: Manual, error-prone system causes over/underpayments; new methods needed for efficiency and transparency.Sales Structure Overview: Simple commission model; sales manager earns 2% override; reps have 10% and 20% commissions.Ideal System Features: Seek automated, accurate visibility for reps; integration with HubSpot for self-service commission data important.QuotaPath Fit: Offers modular components matching needs; features improve reconciliation, commission tracking, and rep motivation.Pricing Details: Growth tier is $525/month for five users; additional users are $35/month; total estimated costs for 15-18 users around $11,760/year.Next Steps: Data scoping call on April 8th for integration details; current compensation plans to be shared before next meeting.Current Commission Process and ChallengesThe team is using a manual, error-prone commission process that limits scalability and transparency (07:50).They currently calculate commissions biweekly using Q Commissions, which requires uploading billing data from Rev IO manually.The process causes overpayments and underpayments because it pays reps based on billed amounts, not actual paid revenue.Sales reps lack visibility into what they earned or when they'll be paid, leading to frequent inquiries and reconciliation headaches.Commission plan changes are difficult to implement, requiring manual spreadsheets and causing logistical issues.Sales and Commission Structure OverviewTheir commission model is relatively simple but needs more flexibility and automation to grow (09:00).Sales manager receives a 2% override on recurring revenue; two sales reps get 10% and 20% commission respectively.New plan offers new reps an extra 50% commission for two months if they hit goals in the first four months.The team comprises one sales manager, seven sales reps, and six agents paid monthly on recurring revenue.Agents receive a 20% cut monthly, but most sales are internal reps, with plans to keep it that way.Ideal Commission System FeaturesThey want to reduce manual work, improve accuracy, and provide clear rep visibility, aiming for HubSpot integration (10:30).Goal is to push commission data and customer account creation into HubSpot for reps to self-serve visibility.Need a system that triggers payments only after invoices are paid to avoid overpaying on multi-month payments.Desire for a biweekly report for payroll that lists commission amounts per rep for easy import.Flexibility is key for future commission plan changes; current tools are too rigid and prone to errors.QuotaPath Platform Demonstration and FitQuotaPath offers automation, transparency, and flexible commission plan design well suited to their needs (22:30).Plans are built with modular components (quotas, tiered rates, accelerators, spiffs) that match their simple commission structure.Ability to assign different commission plans or components based on product lines like VoIP versus Notify by filtering deals in HubSpot.Supports forecasting pipeline commissions, increasing rep motivation by showing potential earnings before deals close.Approval workflows and payout scheduling simplify reconciliation and reduce errors compared to current manual process.Reps get detailed breakdowns of earnings by deal and product, with hyperlinks back to HubSpot for transparency.Integration and Workflow ConsiderationsThe system integrates closely with HubSpot and can support multi-product and multi-role commission tracking (27:00).Payout eligibility is triggered by invoice payment data synced from HubSpot, ensuring reps are paid only for collected revenue.Integration enables mapping deal attributes including term length and product type to tailor commission eligibility.QuotaPath flags and comment features streamline dispute resolution and reduce email back-and-forth.Multiple approval layers can be configured to fit company workflows, improving accuracy and accountability.Pricing and Next StepsPricing is competitive and includes full implementation and ongoing support, with plans for a detailed follow-up call (46:30).Growth tier costs $525 per month or $6,300 annually, covering first five users plus white-glove setup and support.Additional users cost $35 per month each; estimated total for 15-18 users is about $11,760 per year.Standard contract term is two years; discounts and contract break considerations are factors in decision timing.Next step is a data scoping call scheduled for Wednesday the 8th with a sales engineer to define integration details.They will provide current comp plans ahead of next meeting for a tailored proof of concept and demo.Action ItemsJeremy WileyProvide PDF or document of current comp plans to Eric for proof-of-concept build. (51:25)Review existing Q commissions contract terms to determine feasibility/timing of termination. (49:38)Confirm availability for data scoping call next Wednesday. (50:48)Christina EdwardsConfirm availability and participate in next week’s data scoping call. (50:26)Eric McElweeSend meeting recap and proposed next steps summary post-call. (51:12)Schedule and coordinate data scoping call for Wednesday the 8th including sales engineer and Cameron. (50:52)Prepare custom quote and proof-of-concept build based on client comp plans. (44:00)Cameron TaggartParticipate in the data scoping call to assist with integration strategy. (51:01)

ERP System Change Needed: QuickBooks Online can't support growth, necessitating a switch to better manage recurring revenue.Revenue Model Insights: About 90% of revenue is recurring, with a streamlined closing process due to limited billing complications.Campfire’s Unique Features: Selected for strong AI capabilities, suitable for SaaS, improving automation and accounting processes.Demo Planned for Integration: A demonstration on April 8 will evaluate billing efficiency and integration with PNC bank.Reporting Needs Highlighted: Focus on standard metrics like MRR and expense tracking to enhance budget forecasting accuracy.Integration Questions Prioritized: Campfire will quickly address potential blockers, especially concerning Rev IO integration issues.Current ERP and Billing ChallengesThe company is preparing to switch ERP systems to better align with their recurring revenue model and scale operations efficiently (03:05).QuickBooks Online limitations prompt ERP switch as it no longer supports the company’s growth and automation needs, especially for recurring revenue (03:05)Billing is currently handled by Rev IO, focused on telecom state taxes and recurring revenueThe goal is to gain better financial reporting and insights not available in QuickBooksTransition is timed alongside CRM migration from Salesforce to HubSpot to streamline system integrationOperational footprint and complexity are moderate, with no multi-entity or multi-currency accounting requirements but some Canadian business activity (05:23)Parent company exists but operates separately with minimal financial interactionPayroll and AP are outsourced through an accounting firm, reducing internal complexity (06:08)Revenue recognition is primarily recurring, with about 90% recurring revenue and accounts receivable, plus some one-time charges (06:31)The closing process is quick due to limited use of QuickBooks for billing (07:12)No current audits are underway, indicating a less complex compliance environment (07:06)Integration needs focus on Rev IO billing engine, which is essential for half the business; exploring if Campfire can replace or complement existing systems (14:49)If switching ERP, integration with QuickBooks would be unnecessary (08:12)Potential for custom integration with Rev IO if not natively supported by Campfire (15:10)Campfire ERP Platform Features and Strategic FitCampfire was selected for its strong AI-native capabilities and accounting focus tailored for SaaS companies, supporting future growth and automation (10:06).Campfire founded by finance expert John Glasgow after frustration with legacy ERP consolidation and reporting limitations (10:06)The platform is built with input from the Big Four and CPAs to meet accounting needs preciselyCampfire supports multi-entity, multi-currency, fixed assets, leases, and detailed close management (11:36)AI-native ERP with dual AI layers enhances automation and analysis (12:50)Accounting Intelligence automates data classification and cleaning, acting like a junior team memberEmber AI provides senior-level analysis, enabling budgeting, forecasting, and advanced visualizationsFeatures like Flux Analysis help detect unusual transactions with customizable thresholdsStrong integration ecosystem with over 70 native connectors supports HubSpot, payroll, and custom APIs for future-proofing (14:03)Campfire commits to rapid API endpoint delivery for any missing integrations to avoid delaysThe platform’s AI and automation reduce headcount needs, evidenced by a customer growing from $25M to $250M ARR without adding finance staff (15:50)Trusted by SaaS and audited companies, including IPO-ready and publicly traded firms, ensuring robust workflows and compliance (16:23)Implementation Considerations and TimelineThe ERP switch timeline is flexible but must align with the HubSpot CRM rollout to optimize integration and avoid redundant efforts (07:37).Decision dependent on HubSpot contract timing and integration strategy (07:37)The company prefers a one-time ERP switch rather than multiple migrations, emphasizing a “do once and never again” approach (08:21)Demo planned for April 8th at 2pm Eastern to involve bookkeepers and key users for hands-on evaluation (17:49)Key demo focus areas include billing volume, invoice processing, and bank reconciliation efficiency (19:02)Invoicing example: sending 3,500 recurring invoices efficiently in a single batch is criticalIntegration with PNC bank for reconciliation is important to simplify bookkeeping tasksReporting needs center on standard SaaS financial metrics like MRR, forecasts, expense tracking, and variance analysis (19:48)The company aims to improve budget-to-actual forecasting, currently an area for growth (20:09)Acknowledgment that Campfire does not currently support AP, recommending external tools like Ramp or Brex for that function (20:13)Next Steps and Stakeholder EngagementThe company is progressing toward a well-informed ERP decision with a focus on involving operational users and clarifying technical fit (17:20).Scheduling demo with bookkeepers ensures practical evaluation of usability and workflow fit (17:20)Inclusion of Christina Edwards, a recent hire with industry experience, to provide fresh operational insights (18:18)Demo expected to last about 16 minutes, balancing detail and efficiency (17:02)Open communication on specific needs encourages tailored demonstration content (19:02)Emphasis on recurring revenue workflows and ease of invoicing to reduce manual effortReporting and forecast capabilities to be highlighted as core differentiatorsCampfire committed to quick follow-up on integration questions, especially regarding Rev IO, to address potential blockers early (14:58)Meeting closed with clear alignment on next meeting and openness to further questions, signaling a collaborative evaluation process (20:44)Action ItemsjwCoordinate with bookkeepers and internal team to attend Campfire demo on April 8th at 2pm Eastern. (17:20)Prepare specific requirements and questions regarding high-volume invoicing, bank reconciliation, recurring revenue reporting, and budgeting for discussion during the demo. (19:00)Christina EdwardsAttend Campfire demo on April 8th at 2pm Eastern. (18:20)Vasilisa BernatApprove and prepare notes from the meeting. (00:16)Check on Rev IO integration availability and follow up on possibility of custom integration if needed. (14:50)Schedule and send demo invite for April 8th at 2pm Eastern including key users from hellospoke. (18:10)Remain available to address any additional requests or questions about demo content. (20:30)

"account," and "opportunity" identified to ensure a smooth migration process.Data Migration Order: Salesforce data must migrate to HubSpot before QuickBooks integration to prevent data mismatches for commissions.Complex Quoting Process: Current CSV import process for quotes is cumbersome; desire for simpler uploads without dependency on technical support.Sales Funnel Redesign: Redesigning sales funnel stages and definitions is a priority to improve process discipline and metrics clarity.Upcoming Discovery Workshop: A deep dive session is scheduled to analyze current Salesforce usage and outline HubSpot setup and processes.CRM Migration and Data ManagementThe team agreed on a strategic plan to migrate from Salesforce to HubSpot, focusing on cleaning and structuring data for better future integrations.Consensus on Lead and Account Definitions Needed (02:15)Ryan emphasized the need to define terms like "lead," "account," and "opportunity" for a smooth migration.He highlighted current Salesforce issues where leads convert to accounts, but duplicate accounts sometimes result from inconsistent conversions.The team recognized that agreeing on definitions will streamline the rollout and reporting processes.HubSpot Lead Object Utilization Exploration (06:00)Cameron proposed deep discovery on how Salesforce leads currently work and how HubSpot’s lead object could replace or improve this.Billy supported using lead stages in HubSpot for more accurate sales funnel metrics and daily sales management.Ryan acknowledged the need for clear rules on lead progression and measurement, including sales cycle timing and lead value attribution.Data Migration and Integration Order Clarification (27:31)Jeremy stressed the importance of migrating Salesforce data into HubSpot before connecting QuickBooks to avoid data mismatches.The goal is to maintain clean account matching across systems to enable seamless commission processing via QuotaPath.Cameron committed to confirming the migration and integration timeline with Rev Ops lead Kyle.Parent-Child Account Structure and Duplication Issues (42:45)Ryan demonstrated complexity with managing parent property management accounts and multiple child property accounts.Duplicate child accounts are created when quoting, causing confusion and inaccurate reporting.Jeremy and Cameron confirmed the need to enforce parent-child relationships and deduplicate during import and quoting.Sales and Quoting Process OptimizationThe discussion revealed major pain points in quoting workflows, data imports, and system usability, driving a need for automation and simplification.Complex and Error-Prone Mass Quote Uploads (40:24)Ryan showed the cumbersome CSV import process for adding hundreds of property line items to quotes, often resulting in cryptic errors.Jeremy and Ryan discussed issues like mandatory fields, invalid state codes, and format requirements that complicate imports.The team expressed desire for an easier upload method without CSV dependency to reduce errors and reliance on technical support.Quote Amounts and Revenue Metrics Alignment (49:40)Ryan clarified the business focuses on monthly recurring revenue (MRR) for growth measurement, not total contract value.The quoting system automatically updates MRR to reflect final signed amounts, ensuring accurate pipeline and closed-won reporting.Jeremy confirmed reports exist but inconsistencies remain across Salesforce views, highlighting the need for unified reporting post-migration.Proposal Notes and Billing Start Date Automation (51:56)Ryan explained adding deal-specific notes such as free service periods or billing start dates to inform implementation teams.Jeremy emphasized automating billing start date inclusion in orders to reduce manual handoffs and errors.Maintaining some free time fields is important for flexible deal structuring.VoIP Quoting Challenges and Product Complexity (01:00:36)Ryan described VoIP quoting as more complex than software sales, involving setup fees, monthly fees, equipment, and multiple variables.Jeremy noted mass uploads for VoIP are possible but require manual line item adjustments per property, limiting efficiency.Handling combined customers with both VoIP and software products on a single invoice adds further quoting complexity.Process Redesign and Best Practices DevelopmentThe team recognized the need for a joint redesign effort to clarify sales funnel stages, nomenclature, and automation to improve efficiency and metrics.Sales Funnel Redesign as Key Priority (16:21)Billy strongly advocated for redesigning the sales funnel with consistent names, clear stage definitions, and mandatory data requirements to improve process discipline.Ryan agreed but deferred detailed redesign until all stakeholders, including Sarah, can participate.Cameron proposed a structured discovery workshop with Ryan, Sarah, and others to map the entire customer journey from lead to customer in HubSpot.Operational and Automation Enhancements Post-Close (21:37)Ryan and Christina discussed integrating HubSpot with project management tool ClickUp to provide reps visibility into implementation stages of their accounts.This integration aims to improve sales team insight and customer communication on rollout progress.Jeremy noted ClickUp is functioning well currently but is open to alternatives if better integrated with HubSpot.CPQ System Improvement as a Critical Bottleneck (25:31)Jeremy and Ryan identified the current Salesforce CPQ (Configure, Price, Quote) system as inflexible and frequently broken, causing manual intervention and delays.Moving to HubSpot’s native CPQ or a third-party tool like QuotaPath is being explored to empower sales reps to self-serve quotes and speed up the process.The team sees CPQ improvement as urgent due to its ripple effect on operations and sales velocity.Data Integrity and Integration Ownership (28:58)Jeremy stressed the necessity of data integrity during migration to avoid creating a "muddy mess" that complicates billing and commissions downstream.The team agreed HubSpot should act as the central hub integrating billing, quoting, and commission data.Kyle will be consulted to finalize migration sequencing and integration ownership.Technology and Tooling InsightsThe meeting surfaced opportunities to build custom tooling and improve system integrations to handle complex data and improve user experience.Potential for AI-Enabled Data Preprocessing Layer (56:45)Billy proposed building an AI-driven preprocessing tool to handle messy client data formats (PDFs, images, spreadsheets) before import.This layer would validate, refine, and map data automatically, reducing manual errors and support tickets.Ryan welcomed this idea given the frequent poor-quality data received from clients and internal upload errors.HubSpot as a Centralized Platform for Billing and Commissions (24:10)Ryan expressed the vision for HubSpot to unify billing, quoting, and commission workflows, enabling reps to see billing and implementation status in one place.Jeremy confirmed ongoing work to integrate QuickBooks billing data with HubSpot to support commission calculations and reporting.The team acknowledged the need for custom configuration to connect HubSpot with third-party tools like QuotaPath.DocuSign Replacement Exploration (34:20)Jeremy and Cameron discussed plans to move away from DocuSign due to cost increases, evaluating PandaDoc as a potential alternative with better API support.Billy supported this transition and suggested leveraging HubSpot’s CPQ capabilities for generating proposals and agreements.This change aims to reduce costs and improve integration with the CRM and quoting processes.Next Steps and Engagement PlanningThe group outlined immediate next steps centered on discovery, coordination, and milestone setting to ensure smooth migration and process improvement.Discovery Workshop Scheduled for Early Next Week (19:10)Cameron confirmed a deep dive session with Ryan, Sarah (returning around the 8th/9th), and other stakeholders to document current Salesforce usage and desired HubSpot setup.The workshop will cover lead/account/deal definitions, funnel stages, data properties, and criteria for stage transitions.This will guide the rebuilding of processes and data migration, establishing a go-live plan.Coordination with Rev Ops Lead and QuotaPath Vendor (24:54)Cameron will align with Kyle on migration order and integration sequencing, particularly around QuickBooks and commission software.The team will evaluate QuotaPath’s integration capabilities and pricing discounts to finalize the quoting and commission platform choice.Jeremy flagged the need to avoid paying for redundant integrations.Addressing Current Operational Bottlenecks During Transition (18:27)Jeremy emphasized the need to fix immediate Salesforce bottlenecks without incurring high costs, even as a transition to HubSpot approaches.The team agreed on operating both systems temporarily and syncing key data to minimize disruption.Ryan and Jeremy will continue refining quoting and data import workflows in the interim.Further Detailed Discussions Planned (58:00)Ryan indicated the need for additional sessions to cover VoIP quoting and one-property add-ons, which are distinct from the software quoting process shown.Jeremy offered to support follow-up calls and knowledge transfer during Ryan’s upcoming vacation.Cameron expressed confidence that the engagement will significantly ease current pain points and improve sales operations.Action ItemsCameron TaggartSync with Kyle to confirm the order of operations regarding data migration from Salesforce to HubSpot and integration with QuickBooks. (28:58)Schedule a deep-dive session with Sarah, Ryan, and other stakeholders after Sarah’s return to map sales funnel stages, objects, properties, and criteria. (19:10)Confirm project plan including Salesforce current state assessment, CPQ integration, data migration, and crossover/live strategy. (26:15)Evaluate HubSpot CPQ functionality and third-party tools for quoting needs, including integration with QuotaPath. (30:08)Follow up with Jeremy and the team regarding potential replacement or integration strategies for DocuSign with PandaDoc or alternatives. (34:10)Ryan SweeneyPrepare to demo and explain VoIP quoting processes and add-ons for quicker follow-up discussions. (58:50)Continue collaboration with Jeremy and team on sales quoting and quoting data import issues for SaaS and VoIP offerings. (47:11)Jeremy WileyProvide support resolving CSV file format issues during property line-item uploads and quoting. (44:55)Assist in determining best practices for building sales and quoting processes in HubSpot and integrating with existing tools like QuickBooks and commission software. (28:58)Continue exploring alternatives to DocuSign and supporting quoting automation. (34:25)Billy LeighDevelop recommendations for redesigning the sales funnel and sales process with consistent nomenclature and stage entry/exit criteria. (06:30)Explore automation possibilities such as HubSpot Deal Scoring and AI-enabled data preprocessing layers for smooth import of client data from varied file types. (55:38)

SF&gt;HS implementation discovery | HelloSpoke

Migration Strategy: Phased approach to migrate from Salesforce to Hubspot, focusing on lead and deal standardization.Sales Funnel Redesign: Need for clear lead vs. deal definitions to improve pipeline visibility and reduce confusion.CPQ Optimization: Improving CPQ process with automation to eliminate manual intervention and streamline deal closures.Data Integrity Challenges: Address duplicate accounts and maintain accurate parent-child relationships for better reporting.Integration Vision: Centralize data in Hubspot, integrating billing and commission software to enhance visibility and reduce fragmentation.Reporting Focus: Accurate reporting on sales cycle and MRR is essential for growth measurement and performance tracking.Salesforce to Hubspot Migration StrategyThe team agreed on a detailed, phased approach to migrate from Salesforce to Hubspot, focusing heavily on standardizing lead and deal definitions before data transfer (00:38).Cameron Taggart outlined a plan to map Salesforce lead, company, contact, and deal objects to Hubspot equivalents, setting clear entry and exit criteria for each (19:01).This includes defining pipeline stages and key properties to track for consistent reporting and management.The approach will incorporate fixing current pain points while establishing best practices during the rebuild in Hubspot.A cross-over date strategy will be created to ensure smooth go-live and minimal disruption.This plan responds to the current confusion and inconsistent use of lead objects and deal stages described by Ryan Sweeney and Billy Leigh.The team will perform a deep dive with Ryan Sweeney and Sarah (business operations consultant) once Sarah returns (around the 8th or 9th), to finalize process definitions and migration scope (18:50).Jeremy Wiley emphasized the importance of this session to align stakeholders and avoid disrupting current marketing activities on Hubspot.This aligns with the need to prevent duplicative effort and data corruption during the migration.Jeremy Wiley stressed the urgency to move quickly given ongoing operational bottlenecks in Salesforce and commission software (17:27).He raised concerns about running dual systems and the need to avoid paying for fixes in Salesforce that will become obsolete.Cameron confirmed needing to clarify the order of operations with Kyle, prioritizing full Salesforce to Hubspot migration before QuickBooks integration (28:43).Sales Funnel Redesign and Lead ManagementThe team recognized the critical need to redesign the sales funnel, focusing on consistent terminology and stage gating to improve pipeline visibility and management (06:14).Billy Leigh advocated for clear definitions of lead vs. deal stages to avoid distorting pipeline metrics and better track engagement levels (06:20).Leads act as a bridge between contacts and opportunities, capturing early-stage interactions before formal qualification.Strict entrance and exit criteria per stage will ensure data integrity and operational clarity.This redesign aims to streamline workflows and reduce confusion caused by inconsistent lead handling described by Ryan Sweeney.Ryan Sweeney highlighted current pain points with lead duplication, inconsistent conversion practices, and lack of clear sales cycle measurement (04:19).He noted that multiple leads tied to the same company often create duplicate accounts upon conversion.Sales cycle tracking is hindered by unclear definitions of when a lead becomes a qualified opportunity.He requested guidance on best practices and a standardized process to improve data quality and reporting.The team agreed that the lead object in Hubspot differs from Salesforce’s and should be leveraged as a nurturing stage with appropriate drip campaigns (12:41).This would differentiate raw leads from assigned leads who receive more targeted follow-ups.Marketing and sales alignment on lead handling will reduce redundant communication and improve engagement.CPQ and Quoting Process OptimizationImproving the Configure, Price, Quote (CPQ) process emerged as a high priority due to frequent manual intervention, errors, and inefficiencies slowing sales and onboarding (25:03).Jeremy Wiley described Salesforce’s CPQ as inflexible and error-prone, requiring constant manual help for quotes that should be self-service (25:21).This slows deal closure and frustrates sales reps, impacting revenue velocity.He confirmed CPQ improvements are part of the migration scope to Hubspot, including automating quote generation and approvals.Ryan Sweeney demonstrated the complex quoting process involving large property management companies with hundreds of properties, requiring bulk uploads to quotes (30:59).Current CSV upload methods are error-prone, with unclear error messages and manual fixes needed.He requested easier mass upload options without CSV reliance, as well as support for adding single properties quickly.Billy Leigh proposed building an AI-based preprocessing layer to clean and validate data before import, reducing errors and manual rework (55:30).The quote documents automatically update close dates based on DocuSign signatures, streamlining deal closure tracking (33:14).However, the team plans to replace DocuSign with PandaDoc due to cost and dissatisfaction, with Hubspot CPQ potentially handling proposal generation going forward (34:00).Billy Leigh recommended trying to optimize existing tools before switching completely, but recognized Hubspot’s CPQ AI feature is promising (35:07).Data Integrity and Account Hierarchy ChallengesMaintaining clean data with accurate parent-child relationships between management companies and properties is a major concern impacting quoting and reporting accuracy (41:01).Ryan Sweeney and Jeremy Wiley explained how duplicate accounts and sub-accounts arise mostly from inconsistent quoting and account creation during CSV imports (42:36).The system sometimes auto-creates multiple identical sub-accounts when quoting upgrades or new services.They emphasized a need for data scrubbing and validation tools to prevent duplicates and ensure parent-child links are preserved.The team agreed on the importance of associating properties as child accounts under management company parents in Hubspot to reflect real-world structures (41:10).This hierarchy supports accurate reporting on portfolio size and revenue per customer.It also enables reps to track implementation stages and billing status across all properties for a single customer (22:19).Integration and Systems Consolidation VisionThere is a strong strategic vision to centralize sales, billing, commission, and project management data within Hubspot to enhance visibility and reduce tool fragmentation (23:54).Ryan Sweeney emphasized that Hubspot should act as the central hub, integrating with billing engines and commission software to provide reps with complete account status (23:54).He described scenarios where reps need to see rollout and training status for hundreds of properties from within Hubspot.Christina Edwards confirmed plans to integrate ClickUp (project management) data into Hubspot eventually for end-to-end visibility (21:53).Jeremy Wiley is working on a flow to push billing data from Rev (billing engine) into QuickBooks, then into Hubspot, maintaining data alignment across platforms (27:21).He wants to avoid creating mismatched accounts between Salesforce and QuickBooks by completing Salesforce to Hubspot migration first (28:34).This sequencing is critical to establishing a single source of truth for accounts and commissions.The team is actively evaluating QuotaPath commission software, recently negotiating a 70% discount which would reduce costs and facilitate integration with Hubspot (24:35).Jeremy Wiley stressed the need to avoid paying for multiple integrations and prefers routing commission data through Hubspot as a central point.Reporting and Metrics FocusAccurate and relevant reporting around sales cycle, pipeline value, and monthly recurring revenue (MRR) is a key business metric focus (07:24).Ryan Sweeney highlighted confusion from Salesforce presenting both total deal value and MRR, with MRR being the critical metric for business growth measurement (49:29).He requested consistent reporting that reflects real monthly recurring revenue rather than inflated total contract values.This is essential for forecasting, goal tracking, and understanding sales performance.The team acknowledged the need to define when the sales cycle starts and ends to enable meaningful cycle time measurement and better pipeline management (07:30).Consistent stage definitions and data entry rules are prerequisites to accurate cycle metrics.This aligns with the sales funnel redesign and lead management efforts.Jeremy Wiley mentioned existing reports that show monthly closed MRR but noted inconsistencies across different reports and states, indicating a need for data cleanup and unified reporting standards (01:00:00).Action ItemsRyan SweeneyProvide detailed overview and challenges of current Salesforce lead, opportunity, and quoting workflows including mass upload problems for SaaS and VoIP products. (06:13)Share current pain points and desired improvements around lead qualification, quoting accuracy, and billing start dates. (50:33)Collaborate on process mapping and system transition strategy with HelloSpoke team. (01:02:54)Jeremy WileyAssist with technical clarifications regarding CSV upload errors and Salesforce quoting nuances during discovery calls. (44:54)Provide operational insights on quoting, billing workflows, and existing tool usage including ClickUp, QuickBooks, and DocuSign alternatives. (20:40)Support process alignment and data integrity considerations in Salesforce to HubSpot migration. (28:28)Billy LeighAdvise on sales funnel redesign, consistent nomenclature, and HubSpot deal pipeline scoring features like Deal Score. (06:13)Propose AI-powered preprocessing layers for handling varied customer portfolio data formats to automate uploads and reduce errors. (55:00)Christina EdwardsCollaborate on integrating ClickUp and HubSpot for project management visibility into customer accounts. (21:19)Support marketing and operations alignment on lead nurturing and communication workflows. (12:41)Cameron TaggartSync with Kyle on order of operations for Salesforce to HubSpot migration and data integration, including QuickBooks and Quotapath interfaces. (00:29)Coordinate deep dive session with Ryan, Sarah, and stakeholders post-Sarah’s return to finalize sales funnel redesign and pipeline definitions. (18:39)Assess and recommend CPQ solutions within HubSpot and third-party tools, including configuring integrations with Quotapath if selected. (00:30)

to reduce manual handoffs and errors.Maintaining some free time fields is important for flexible deal structuring.VoIP Quoting Challenges and Product Complexity (01:00:36)Ryan described VoIP quoting as more complex than software sales, involving setup fees, monthly fees, equipment, and multiple variables.Jeremy noted mass uploads for VoIP are possible but require manual line item adjustments per property, limiting efficiency.Handling combined customers with both VoIP and software products on a single invoice adds further quoting complexity.Process Redesign and Best Practices DevelopmentThe team recognized the need for a joint redesign effort to clarify sales funnel stages, nomenclature, and automation to improve efficiency and metrics.Sales Funnel Redesign as Key Priority (16:21)Billy strongly advocated for redesigning the sales funnel with consistent names, clear stage definitions, and mandatory data requirements to improve process discipline.Ryan agreed but deferred detailed redesign until all stakeholders, including Sarah, can participate.Cameron proposed a structured discovery workshop with Ryan, Sarah, and others to map the entire customer journey from lead to customer in HubSpot.Operational and Automation Enhancements Post-Close (21:37)Ryan and Christina discussed integrating HubSpot with project management tool ClickUp to provide reps visibility into implementation stages of their accounts.This integration aims to improve sales team insight and customer communication on rollout progress.Jeremy noted ClickUp is functioning well currently but is open to alternatives if better integrated with HubSpot.CPQ System Improvement as a Critical Bottleneck (25:31)Jeremy and Ryan identified the current Salesforce CPQ (Configure, Price, Quote) system as inflexible and frequently broken, causing manual intervention and delays.Moving to HubSpot’s native CPQ or a third-party tool like QuotaPath is being explored to empower sales reps to self-serve quotes and speed up the process.The team sees CPQ improvement as urgent due to its ripple effect on operations and sales velocity.Data Integrity and Integration Ownership (28:58)Jeremy stressed the necessity of data integrity during migration to avoid creating a "muddy mess" that complicates billing and commissions downstream.The team agreed HubSpot should act as the central hub integrating billing, quoting, and commission data.Kyle will be consulted to finalize migration sequencing and integration ownership.Technology and Tooling InsightsThe meeting surfaced opportunities to build custom tooling and improve system integrations to handle complex data and improve user experience.Potential for AI-Enabled Data Preprocessing Layer (56:45)Billy proposed building an AI-driven preprocessing tool to handle messy client data formats (PDFs, images, spreadsheets) before import.This layer would validate, refine, and map data automatically, reducing manual errors and support tickets.Ryan welcomed this idea given the frequent poor-quality data received from clients and internal upload errors.HubSpot as a Centralized Platform for Billing and Commissions (24:10)Ryan expressed the vision for HubSpot to unify billing, quoting, and commission workflows, enabling reps to see billing and implementation status in one place.Jeremy confirmed ongoing work to integrate QuickBooks billing data with HubSpot to support commission calculations and reporting.The team acknowledged the need for custom configuration to connect HubSpot with third-party tools like QuotaPath.DocuSign Replacement Exploration (34:20)Jeremy and Cameron discussed plans to move away from DocuSign due to cost increases, evaluating PandaDoc as a potential alternative with better API support.Billy supported this transition and suggested leveraging HubSpot’s CPQ capabilities for generating proposals and agreements.This change aims to reduce costs and improve integration with the CRM and quoting processes.Next Steps and Engagement PlanningThe group outlined immediate next steps centered on discovery, coordination, and milestone setting to ensure smooth migration and process improvement.Discovery Workshop Scheduled for Early Next Week (19:10)Cameron confirmed a deep dive session with Ryan, Sarah (returning around the 8th/9th), and other stakeholders to document current Salesforce usage and desired HubSpot setup.The workshop will cover lead/account/deal definitions, funnel stages, data properties, and criteria for stage transitions.This will guide the rebuilding of processes and data migration, establishing a go-live plan.Coordination with Rev Ops Lead and QuotaPath Vendor (24:54)Cameron will align with Kyle on migration order and integration sequencing, particularly around QuickBooks and commission software.The team will evaluate QuotaPath’s integration capabilities and pricing discounts to finalize the quoting and commission platform choice.Jeremy flagged the need to avoid paying for redundant integrations.Addressing Current Operational Bottlenecks During Transition (18:27)Jeremy emphasized the need to fix immediate Salesforce bottlenecks without incurring high costs, even as a transition to HubSpot approaches.The team agreed on operating both systems temporarily and syncing key data to minimize disruption.Ryan and Jeremy will continue refining quoting and data import workflows in the interim.Further Detailed Discussions Planned (58:00)Ryan indicated the need for additional sessions to cover VoIP quoting and one-property add-ons, which are distinct from the software quoting process shown.Jeremy offered to support follow-up calls and knowledge transfer during Ryan’s upcoming vacation.Cameron expressed confidence that the engagement will significantly ease current pain points and improve sales operations.Action ItemsCameron TaggartSync with Kyle to confirm the order of operations regarding data migration from Salesforce to HubSpot and integration with QuickBooks. (28:58)Schedule a deep-dive session with Sarah, Ryan, and other stakeholders after Sarah’s return to map sales funnel stages, objects, properties, and criteria. (19:10)Confirm project plan including Salesforce current state assessment, CPQ integration, data migration, and crossover/live strategy. (26:15)Evaluate HubSpot CPQ functionality and third-party tools for quoting needs, including integration with QuotaPath. (30:08)Follow up with Jeremy and the team regarding potential replacement or integration strategies for DocuSign with PandaDoc or alternatives. (34:10)Ryan SweeneyPrepare to demo and explain VoIP quoting processes and add-ons for quicker follow-up discussions. (58:50)Continue collaboration with Jeremy and team on sales quoting and quoting data import issues for SaaS and VoIP offerings. (47:11)Jeremy WileyProvide support resolving CSV file format issues during property line-item uploads and quoting. (44:55)Assist in determining best practices for building sales and quoting processes in HubSpot and integrating with existing tools like QuickBooks and commission software. (28:58)Continue exploring alternatives to DocuSign and supporting quoting automation. (34:25)Billy LeighDevelop recommendations for redesigning the sales funnel and sales process with consistent nomenclature and stage entry/exit criteria. (06:30)Explore automation possibilities such as HubSpot Deal Scoring and AI-enabled data preprocessing layers for smooth import of client data from varied file types. (55:38)

SF→HS Implementation Discovery | HelloSpoke — Apr 2, 2026Duration: 64 minAttendees (HelloSpoke): Jeremy Wiley, Ryan Sweeney, Christina EdwardsAttendees (Sayer): Cameron Taggart, Kyle H, Billy LeighSummaryMigration strategy confirmed: Salesforce data migrates to HubSpot before QuickBooks integration to avoid data mismatches for commissionsLead/account/opportunity definitions: Need alignment — duplicate accounts from inconsistent SF conversions identified as major pain pointCPQ bottleneck: Current SF CPQ is broken — cumbersome CSV imports, cryptic errors. Evaluating HubSpot CPQ vs. QuotaPathSales funnel redesign: Priority — consistent nomenclature, stage definitions, entry/exit criteria needed before migrationIntegration architecture: HubSpot as central hub connecting QuickBooks, QuotaPath (commissions), ClickUp, PandaDoc (DocuSign replacement)DecisionsSF data migrates first, then QB integrationDiscovery workshop with Sarah, Ryan, stakeholders ~Apr 8-9 to map funnel stages, objects, propertiesDocuSign replacement under evaluation (PandaDoc front-runner)Next StepsCameron: Confirm migration order with Kyle, schedule deep-dive workshopBilly: Develop sales funnel redesign recommendations with stage entry/exit criteriaRyan: Prep VoIP quoting walkthrough for follow-up sessionChristina: Facilitate internal transition coordination

**2026-04-08T17:00:00Z**

QuotaPath &lt;&gt; HelloSpoke: Data Scoping
    
        
            
            April 8, 2026
            12:00 PM CDT
            

            
            51 mins
            
        

        
        
            
                
                eric.mcelwee@quotapath.com
                
                alex.lindsey@quotapath.com
                
                cameron@gosayer.com
                
                jwiley@hellospoke.com
                
                kyle@gosayer.com
                
                weston.b@gosayer.com
                
                tim.h@gosayer.com
                
                cedwards@hellospoke.com
                
                kyle.h@gosayer.com
                
            
        
        

        
        
            View Meeting Recording
        
        
    
    
    

Compensation Plans: Four plans reviewed focusing on automated commission calculations linked to MRR and bonuses.Tiered Commission Structure: 15% commission up to $2,500 MRR, increasing to 20% for amounts above that threshold.Data Integration: Invoice data flows from Rev IO to QuickBooks Online and HubSpot for centralized tracking.Commission Splits: Handled at the product level; large commissions may be paid in installments with manual overrides.Implementation Timeline: Expected within 30 to 60 days, with preliminary pricing around $9,000 annually, 25% off list price.Compensation Plan Design and LogicThe team reviewed four compensation plans built in QuotaPath, focusing on automating commission calculations tied to monthly recurring revenue (MRR) and bonuses. (05:08)Agent Plan with Tiered Commission Rates details a 15% commission up to $2,500 MRR monthly, retroactively increasing to 20% on all MRR above $2,500 (08:57)This structure adjusts commission rates monthly based on performance relative to the $2,500 MRR pool target.Jeremy confirmed commissions reset monthly if MRR dips below thresholds, ensuring accurate incentive alignment.The plan accommodates varying agent goals and can be customized per rep.This tiered approach encourages agents to exceed targets by rewarding higher sales volumes retroactively.Sales Rep Plan with Annual Bonus Trigger linked to a $36,000 yearly target, with bonuses applied retroactively once the goal is met (09:46)The bonus currently operates annually but may shift to quarterly next year; this affects payout timing.Eric and Jeremy agreed to separate the quota target from the bonus component to reflect annual tracking accurately.The system supports multiple plans for reps at different seniority levels, allowing tiered goal setting.This structure motivates sustained annual performance while offering flexibility for future cadence changes.Sales Manager and Override Plans include personal sales commissions at 10% and overrides on team sales at 2-3% depending on the manager (29:40)Ryan Sweeney’s sales manager plan pays 2% on team sales and 10% on personal sales; Ryan Moorman’s override plan pays 3% on team sales.Commissions are filtered by deal ownership and invoice dates to handle timing and tail-offs correctly.No test data was processed for these plans yet, but the logic is straightforward and consistent with hierarchical compensation models.These override structures incentivize managers to support their teams while rewarding personal contributions.Automated Handling of Monthly and Annual Commission Logic enables retroactive rate changes and bonus eligibility tracking based on deal close dates (10:41)The system automatically applies different commission rates for deals closing pre- and post-June, respecting contract timing.Retroactivity ensures commissions are adjusted correctly once sales exceed targets or annual bonuses kick in.This automation reduces manual calculation errors and streamlines payout accuracy.The approach supports complex commission scenarios without manual overrides, improving operational efficiency.Data Integration and Invoice TrackingThe team discussed integrating billing and invoice data from Rev IO, QuickBooks Online, and HubSpot to support commission calculations based on actual payments. (11:59)Invoice Data Flow Plan involves pushing billing data from Rev IO to QuickBooks Online, then into HubSpot, enabling centralized commission tracking (12:14)Jeremy explained this multi-step integration aligns with telecom billing complexity and existing workflows.HubSpot will hold summarized invoice and payment information visible to sales reps, with full invoice details optionally imported.This integration strategy balances data completeness with practical visibility for reps and admins.The setup aims to maintain data accuracy while minimizing duplicate effort between systems.Line Item-Level Invoice Detail Needed for Splits and Product Differentiation to distinguish commissions on different product types like SaaS vs. VoIP (22:00)Jeremy showed typical invoices with line items categorized by product types; these details are crucial for correct commission splits.Alex emphasized the importance of linking line items or invoices to deals with unique IDs to ensure accurate payout attribution.The team agreed to create categories or metadata fields in HubSpot to tag invoice line items by product type.This granular data enables split commissions and spiffs for different product sales, supporting multi-product sales teams.Custom Objects and API Integration proposed to track usage or billing events tied to deals, providing required granularity and flexibility (18:11)Weston referenced a similar use case with a custom object tracking energy usage monthly, tied to deals for commission triggers.The team considers APIs to pull line item data directly from Rev IO to avoid manual invoice parsing.Linking usage or invoice line items to deals ensures clean data joins for commission calculations.This approach supports recurring billing events and complex billing scenarios typical in telecom.Challenges with Deal IDs in Rev IO and Multiple Deals per Account require further investigation to ensure accurate matching (15:24)Jeremy noted Rev IO currently tracks accounts but not deal IDs, complicating one-to-one joins between invoices and deals.Multiple deals per customer due to upgrades or add-ons further complicate linking.Options include enriching invoice data with deal IDs or using invoice objects standalone with owner and close date metadata.The team plans additional discovery sessions to finalize integration architecture.Commission Splits and Special CasesThe handling of split commissions and exceptions like large one-off deals were clarified for smooth payout operations. (20:29)Split Commissions Occur Primarily on VoIP Deals with Multiple Salespeople where one rep handles SaaS and another VoIP (20:44)Splits are managed at the product or line item level rather than across entire deals.The volume of such splits is small, simplifying configuration.Customer success spiffs are paid separately as one-time payments, not commission splits.This approach limits complexity while fairly compensating joint sales efforts.Large One-Off Commissions Can Be Flagged and Paid in Installments with manual override and payout scheduling options (31:06)Jeremy confirmed large commissions like the Widener contract are typically paid as lump sums outside the system.Alex recommended filtering such deals by deal type or stage to exclude them from normal automated payouts.QuotaPath supports installment payments within the payout screen for large sums.This flexibility prevents double payments and supports cash flow considerations.Approval and Flagging Workflow for Commission Discrepancies ensures accuracy and accountability before payouts (36:57)Reps can flag earnings issues or unpaid invoices directly in QuotaPath.Admins receive notifications and can review flagged items before approving payouts.This transparent feedback loop reduces errors and enhances trust in commissions.It also supports quick resolution of disputes or data issues.User Experience and Reporting for Sales Reps and AdminsThe platform provides sales reps and admins with tailored views to track earnings, payment status, and team performance easily. (35:05)Sales Reps See Their Own Earnings, Deal Details, and Payment History with direct links to HubSpot records for transparency (35:14)Reps can view recent deals and invoices with commission breakdowns and explanations generated by AI.They can filter payment history by month or search by customer to track full payment and commission timelines.Only paid invoices appear, providing clear confirmation of earned commissions.This visibility addresses common rep questions about payment status and earnings.Leaderboards and Team Attainment Views foster healthy competition and team motivation (34:51)Teams and leaderboards can be configured within QuotaPath for agents and sales reps.Reps see their attainment as a percentage of monthly targets, encouraging goal pursuit.Privacy is maintained by restricting reps to their own commission data while allowing visibility of relative performance.This feature aims to boost engagement and sales productivity.Admin Side Includes Approval Queues and Payout Scheduling with exportable CSV files for payroll integration (40:15)Admins review commissions awaiting approval, verify calculations, and approve or flag concerns.Payouts are scheduled and exported as CSV files compatible with payroll systems.The system supports installment payouts and deal-level granularity.This streamlines commission disbursement and maintains audit trails.Search and Filter Functions Enhance Usability for Both Reps and Admins enabling fast access to relevant commission data (38:04)Search by customer name or deal allows reps to quickly find payment records.Filters by month or payment status help reps and admins analyze earnings and outstanding invoices.This reduces administrative burden and improves user confidence in commission tracking.It aligns with the need for transparent, timely sales compensation information.Implementation Timeline, Pricing, and Next StepsThe group discussed integration complexities, timeline expectations, pricing ballpark, and scheduling follow-up meetings to finalize details. (44:30)Implementation Expected Within 30 to 60 Days depending on data cleanliness and integration complexity (47:08)The biggest phase is validation, running QuotaPath in parallel with existing systems.Early stages include building commission plans and mapping data flows alongside HubSpot implementation.Jeremy emphasized potential delays due to the HubSpot rollout and integration with Rev IO and QuickBooks.The team plans to coordinate timelines to avoid paying for unused licenses.Preliminary Pricing Estimate Around $9,000 Annually with about 25% off list price mentioned as a ballpark (06:18)Eric will provide a formal quote in the next call after internal approvals.Payment terms such as net 30 or net 60 may be discussed to ease cash flow.Pricing discussion is scheduled for Tuesday at 1:30 PM Eastern.This aligns with the upcoming contract expiration with Q commissions in September.Sandbox Access Offered for Hands-On Testing and Validation enabling the team to explore plans and imports (43:39)Jeremy requested a sandbox to try imports and manipulate plans before full commitment.Eric agreed to provide access for a few days to support hands-on evaluation.This will help the team gain confidence in the platform’s fit and usability.Early experimentation is important to smooth adoption and implementation.Further Technical Discovery Calls Planned for Rev IO and HubSpot Integration to finalize API usage and data architecture (27:19)Weston suggested an additional call to dive deeper into Rev IO APIs and HubSpot custom objects.The goal is to architect a robust data flow supporting commission logic and reporting.This will clarify how billing data is ingested and linked to deals for accurate commissions.These technical sessions are critical to minimizing surprises during rollout.Action ItemsEric McElweeAdd HelloSpoke team to a QuotaPath sandbox environment to allow hands-on trial of compensation plans. (43:39)Prepare and send detailed pricing and discount proposal for HelloSpoke. (48:42)Discuss and confirm potential payment term flexibility to support HelloSpoke’s implementation timeline. (47:52)Schedule next meeting for pricing and discount discussion on Tuesday at 1:30 PM Eastern time. (48:42)Jeremy WileyInvestigate feasibility of including deal ID linkage in Rev IO or alternative data structures to support invoice-to-deal mapping in HubSpot. (16:13)Provide product line categorization for invoice line items to aid commission logic implementation. (25:06)Coordinate internally on HubSpot integration timeline and explore interim manual import options for QuotaPath usage. (44:30)Weston BakerLead further discovery on Rev IO API capabilities and coordinate with Kyle and QuotaPath to architect data flow into HubSpot custom objects. (27:18)Alex LindseySupport technical requirements discussion around invoice and line item data structure and provide insights on commission calculation logic and payout capabilities. (11:46)Christina EdwardsAssist with exception flagging rules and commission approval workflows within QuotaPath. (31:06)
