Custom Reporting Features: Campfire offers flexible reporting with drag-and-drop functionality and unlimited tagging for detailed financial insights.AI-Driven Analytics: Native language queries generate accurate cash flow forecasts and variance analyses while reducing AI risks.Rev IO Integration: Manages high-volume telecom invoices, generating 4,000-5,000 monthly, supporting flexible invoicing setups.Automated Workflows: Integrated month-end close process with task tracking and AI-augmented checks streamlines accounting efforts.Sandbox Environment: Follow-up session to explore Campfire's platform with user data; access provided for hands-on testing.Strategic Focus: Transition from legacy tools emphasizes improving financial automation, reporting accuracy, and audit readiness in telecommunications.Product Demonstration and Reporting CapabilitiesThe demo showcased Campfire’s flexible reporting tools and AI-powered analytics designed to improve financial visibility and customization.Custom and Standard Reporting Overview demonstrated with live examples of income statements, P&amp;L customization, and vendor expense reports (10:26)Reports use drag-and-drop features and support unlimited tagging for departments, projects, or locations.JW noted their current lack of tagging but expressed interest due to reporting improvement needs.Reports can be exported to Excel, preserving formatting and formulas, accommodating Microsoft Excel users.Scheduling of reports for regular delivery was confirmed, supporting weekly or monthly distribution.AI-Driven Analytics and Forecasting introduced with native language queries generating cash flow forecasts and variance analyses with detailed commentary (21:33)AI runs Python code directly on GL data to ensure accuracy, reducing typical AI hallucination risks.Cole emphasized human review remains part of the process to maintain data integrity.Flux and variance analyses highlight expense trends and provide actionable insights to non-accountants.Sandbox environments will be provided for hands-on user exploration and validation of AI outputs.Integration with Third-Party Tools and Platforms includes Power BI via data lakes and Google Sheets with refreshable data feeds (28:13)Campfire covers about 80% of FP&amp;A needs internally, with direct integrations available for advanced FP&amp;A tools like Abacom and Pigment.This integration strategy balances in-house capabilities with external specialized platforms.User Experience and Interaction designed for interactivity and ease, with drill-down capability from summarized reports to transaction details (19:37)Custom roles and permissioning support report access control, ensuring data security and appropriate segregation of duties.Team encouraged interactive demo participation to tailor the experience to their needs.Revenue Management and CRM IntegrationCampfire’s revenue automation and CRM integration provide a streamlined order-to-cash process tailored to telecom billing and contract complexity.Rev IO Integration Strategy centers on ingesting billing data and invoices from Rev IO, with two-way sync for payments and customer accounts (06:46)JW underscored Rev IO’s role as the telecom billing engine managing taxes and generating approximately 4,000-5,000 invoices monthly.Campfire can import these invoices, map GL accounts, and handle revenue recognition without disrupting existing billing workflows.Flexibility to either continue sending invoices from Rev IO or shift invoicing fully to Campfire was discussed, prioritizing low implementation lift.Contract and Subscription Revenue Recognition demonstrated via Salesforce and HubSpot integration with automatic creation of revenue schedules at opportunity close (39:34)Cole showed how contracts move from CRM to Campfire, with prorated revenue recognition schedules supporting monthly billing.Usage-based billing and one-time fees like installation or setup charges are supported, aligning with existing business models.Custom fields and tagging on invoices and deals allow linking to commissions and detailed product-level reporting.Order to Cash and Payment Processing options include ACH, lockbox checks, online portals, and Stripe links for customer payments (45:03)JW explained most customers pay by check or ACH, with a minority using online portals.Campfire supports multiple payment methods and provides customer portals for invoice visibility.Payment matching automation and Slack/email notifications help reconcile payments efficiently.Month-End Close and Workflow AutomationCampfire offers integrated month-end close workflows with task assignments, review layers, and AI-augmented automation to reduce manual effort.Close Checklist and Task Management built into the ERP system provide transparency and accountability for closing tasks (46:22)Tasks can be assigned to specific users with completion tracking and review steps to support segregation of duties.Automated journal entries for fixed asset depreciation and prepaid amortization help streamline recurring accounting processes.Flux analysis and other AI-driven checks can be added to the checklist for enhanced monthly close rigor.Current Closing Process vs Campfire’s Solution compared, highlighting manual QuickBooks and spreadsheet-based workflows currently in use (48:59)JW described the manual nature of current close processes and transition challenges with new staff.Campfire’s integrated approach aims to reduce reliance on disconnected tools and improve audit readiness.The system’s audit trail and workflow approvals were developed with input from Big Four auditors.Automation of Manual Workflows discussed, with opportunities to automate report distribution and approval triggers based on thresholds or exceptions (27:30)AI agents monitor transactions for anomalies, duplicates, and unusual values, sending Slack alerts for review.Custom agents can be created to automate monthly journal entries or other repetitive tasks, reducing manual workload.Accounts Payable and Payment AutomationCampfire supports AP processes primarily through manual bill uploads and light automation, suitable for companies with moderate invoice volumes.AP Volume and Process Fit clarified with JW estimating 30 to 50 bills per month, indicating moderate transaction volume (50:52)Campfire allows bill upload via UI or CSV, supporting bulk uploads to simplify entry.Payment application can be done individually or in bulk, including automated matching of bank outflows to open bills.Integration with AP Tools such as Ramp and Brex is available but optional based on customer needs (50:39)JW confirmed they do not currently use those tools extensively, so direct upload and payment features suffice.Notifications and review workflows support payment accuracy and fraud detection.Payment Application and Matching enhanced by AI agents that suggest matches and notify users via Slack and email for approval (51:54)Multiple ways to mark bills paid, improving flexibility for different payment types and workflows.This automation reduces manual reconciliation and speeds up AP processing.Next Steps and Customer EngagementThe team agreed on a follow-up sandbox session to deepen product familiarity and address detailed operational questions.Sandbox Environment Setup will allow HelloSpoke users to explore Campfire’s platform with their own or dummy data for hands-on testing (25:15)JW requested this to better understand and validate system capabilities.Cole and Vasilisa committed to providing access and support for onboarding.Follow-Up Demo Scheduled for Wednesday at 1:30 PM Eastern to review sandbox experience and answer outstanding questions (54:24)This session will focus on detailed use cases and operational fit.Communication via Slack channel will be established to streamline questions and updates.Open Communication Channels with Cole and Vasilisa via email and Slack ensure ongoing support during the evaluation phase (55:08)JW confirmed Slack use, facilitating quick issue resolution and collaboration.The team expressed positive initial impressions and readiness to proceed with deeper engagement.Strategic Alignment and Business ContextThe discussion reflected a strategic focus on improving financial automation, reporting accuracy, and audit readiness while managing existing telecom billing complexity.Transition from Legacy Tools like QuickBooks and manual spreadsheets underlines the need for integrated ERP and AI capabilities (49:08)Staff turnover and onboarding new finance personnel increase the urgency for streamlined workflows.Campfire’s AI and workflow features address gaps in reporting, close management, and compliance.Revenue Recognition Evolution aligns with business plans to move from month-to-month to longer-term contracts (33:47)Campfire’s flexible revenue schedules and deferred revenue management support future growth strategies.Integration with CRM systems ensures contract data flows seamlessly into finance operations.Reporting and Visibility Improvements sought by HelloSpoke drive interest in custom tagging, scheduled reports, and AI variance analysis (12:39)Enhanced financial insights will help non-accountants better understand business performance.The ability to customize and automate reports reduces manual effort and increases accuracy.Market Positioning and Competitive Differentiation for Campfire rests on AI integration, seamless connectivity to billing systems, and user-friendly reporting (31:00)Running Python code behind AI queries ensures trustworthy outputs versus generic chatbots.The platform’s modularity supports gradual adoption aligned with customer operational maturity and complexity.Action ItemsCole Sanuik and Vasilisa BernatCreate and manage Slack communication channel for ongoing project support and collaboration between Campfire and HelloSpoke teams. (55:08)HelloSpoke Team (, Monika, Aaron)Review Campfire sandbox environment and prepare questions for the follow-up session to optimize HelloSpoke's implementation. (54:05)Vasilisa BernatCommunicate scheduling constraints and ensure smooth handoff during demo presentation from Vasilisa Bernat to Cole Sanuik. (04:30)Cole SanuikSchedule and send sandbox environment access to HelloSpoke team for hands-on trial of Campfire ERP features. (54:57)Schedule follow-up meeting to review sandbox usage and answer HelloSpoke questions, set for Wednesday at 1:30 PM Eastern. (54:49)Prepare demos and documentation highlighting key features such as Rev IO integration, custom tagging, AI capabilities, and month-end close automation tailored for HelloSpoke. Integration Plan: Rev IO, QuickBooks, HubSpot, and QuotaPath will be integrated for streamlined commission calculations and billing accuracy.Automation Checks: Manual sign-off process for billing remains to ensure accuracy before account creation in Rev IO, preserving integrity.Dynamic Pricing Updates: HubSpot will manage price changes and add-ons, ensuring synchronized updates across all platforms without manual entry.Visibility Enhancement: Short-term sync from ClickUp to HubSpot will improve sales rep awareness of implementation status without disrupting current tools.Reporting Development: Follow-up sessions planned to refine sales dashboards, integrating actuals with targets for improved performance tracking.Operational Improvements: Automated reminders and training will reduce delays and redundant inquiries, fostering better collaboration and accountability.Commission and Billing IntegrationThe team decided to streamline commission calculation and billing by integrating Rev IO, QuickBooks, HubSpot, and QuotaPath to reduce manual steps and improve transparency (00:00).Commission calculation automation plan centers on feeding billing data from Rev IO into HubSpot via QuickBooks, which then links to QuotaPath for commission payouts (02:01)Jeremy explained that Rev IO does not currently flow into Salesforce but integrates with QuickBooks for billing reconciliationQuickBooks will push customer and invoice data into HubSpot using existing integrations, ensuring billing and payments stay syncedThe deal object in HubSpot will serve as the source for commission calculations in QuotaPath, improving visibility for sales reps and reducing queriesThis integration aims to provide a single source of truth for billing and commissions, eliminating confusion and errorsManual sign-off process for billing records will remain to ensure accuracy before accounts are created in Rev IO, protecting billing integrity (11:58)Bookkeepers will verify account data before automatic creation in Rev and QuickBooks, adding a control layerOnce signed off, data flow will automate invoice generation, payments, and commission triggersThis approach balances automation with necessary human checks to avoid errorsFlexible handling of price changes and add-ons will originate primarily from HubSpot as the source of truth, then propagate to Rev and QuickBooks (16:00)Price changes can occur at sale or renewal, including add-ons like AI features, requiring dynamic updatesJeremy emphasized avoiding manual updates in multiple systems to reduce errors and workloadHubSpot will push billing records or price overrides to Rev, triggering synchronized updatesThis method supports evolving pricing models while maintaining consistent billing and commission dataAlerting and escalation workflows will be implemented in HubSpot to prevent delays in billing approvals and order processing (21:27)Sara highlighted the need for automated reminders and escalation if tasks linger beyond defined thresholdsWeston described options like Slack or email notifications and task completion triggers to enforce accountabilityManager-level escalation will ensure unresolved issues are addressed promptly, improving operational flowThese controls aim to minimize bottlenecks and reduce manual follow-upSales and Implementation Workflow VisibilityThe team discussed how to provide sales reps visibility into implementation status without disrupting existing operational tools like ClickUp (08:03).Current process uses ClickUp for implementation tracking, with limited sales access but little active monitoring by sales reps (07:08)After deal closure, orders are created in Salesforce, and ClickUp manages onboarding and training tasksBilling records trigger bookkeeping and invoicing but do not currently reflect implementation phase in HubSpotJeremy noted sales rarely log into ClickUp, leaving a gap in status visibilityShort-term solution to sync implementation status from ClickUp to HubSpot by importing key status fields to show simple indicators like "In Progress" (09:56)Christina and Jeremy agreed that a few dependent fields reflecting active statuses would suffice for nowThis minimal integration would reduce calls to implementation while keeping sales informedThe team acknowledged full lifecycle integration into HubSpot is a longer-term goal but not urgentDiscussion on possible HubSpot Service Hub usage for implementation tracking concluded it may not fit current needs since the process is more checklist-driven than service ticket-based (10:44)Jeremy and Christina prefer to keep using ClickUp since it works well and isn’t brokenThe focus is on incremental improvements to keep stakeholders informed without adding complexityAgreement on training and communication improvements to encourage sales reps to trust status indicators and reduce unnecessary inquiries (09:04)Sara emphasized training sales on using status updates to avoid overburdening the implementation teamClear expectations on status updates and target dates will help sales manage their outreachReporting and Dashboard EnhancementsThe team plans a follow-up session to refine sales dashboards and reporting to improve accuracy and trend visibility beyond current capabilities (28:15).Sara and Ryan will lead the effort to develop more robust sales reporting integrating actuals with targets and adding trending analysis (28:42)Jeremy, who created the current reports, will join to provide historical context and data insightsThe goal is to build dashboards that give deeper, more accurate views of sales performanceThis effort will align reporting to better support sales management and forecasting needsScheduling follow-up meetings for detailed walkthroughs was agreed, with a preference for Tuesday at 11 AM Central to accommodate team availability (29:20)Cameron coordinated the scheduling and email communication to include all relevant stakeholdersThis session will help clarify dashboard requirements and technical implementation plansTechnical Integration and System PlanningThe team reviewed technical aspects and sandbox availability to support integration development between Rev IO, HubSpot, and other systems (24:20).Rev IO supports webhooks for events like invoice creation, enabling real-time data pushes to other systems (24:40)Jeremy confirmed access to a Rev IO sandbox environment for testing integrationsHubSpot also has sandbox environments to develop and validate workflows before production deploymentChallenges with Salesforce sandbox access were noted, requiring troubleshootingHubSpot’s product and order management capabilities will be leveraged to manage product pricing, add-ons, and billing triggers (18:43)Weston recommended using HubSpot as the single source for products and pricing to simplify updatesLine items in HubSpot can represent base products and add-ons, supporting flexible billing scenariosThis approach aligns with the planned flow from HubSpot to Rev and QuickBooksIntegration design will include manual approval steps and alerting to maintain billing accuracy and operational control (20:28)Jeremy emphasized the need for bookkeeper sign-off on billing records before automated account creationAlerting mechanisms will notify responsible parties of pending tasks or exceptionsThese controls ensure that automation does not sacrifice accuracy or oversightThe team agreed on the importance of clear handoff processes and data requirements before building integrations (23:04)Weston stressed documenting required data fields and approval steps as a "blueprint" before developmentThis document will serve as training and enablement material for the teamEstablishing this foundation reduces rework and clarifies accountabilityProcess Improvements and Operational ControlsThe meeting highlighted the need for improved operational processes to reduce manual work and increase transparency across billing, commissions, and implementation (21:44).Automated task reminders and escalation workflows will reduce delays and missed approvals (21:44)Setting age-based triggers for tasks or deal stages will generate notifications or escalate to managersThis ensures timely processing and accountability for open billing and implementation itemsThe system will flag issues before they impact customer experience or commission payoutsManual interventions remain critical for key approval points to avoid errors in billing and commission calculations (11:58)Bookkeepers will approve billing records before accounts are created in Rev and QuickBooksThis manual checkpoint prevents incorrect billing data from propagating downstreamThe balance of automation and controls supports operational efficiency without riskTraining sales teams and other stakeholders on new tools and status updates will reduce redundant contacts and confusion (09:04)Clear communication on where to check status and what information is available will streamline interactionsReducing unnecessary calls to implementation frees up resources for onboarding and supportThis cultural shift will improve internal collaboration and customer serviceRegular reporting and review of pending tasks will keep processes on track (21:27)Weekly reports will highlight stuck items and enable proactive managementThis transparency helps identify bottlenecks and areas for continuous improvementCombining alerts with reporting creates a comprehensive operational oversight frameworkAction ItemsJeremy WileyProvide detailed compensation plan documentation to Sara and fill in missing parts after Ryan returns to clarify commission structures and support planning. (06:26)Clarify details regarding usage-based commissions and confirm the sales representatives' payout structure to ensure accurate commission calculations. (05:59)Prepare and conduct a demonstration of Rev IO’s billing and account creation flow to enhance technical understanding among the team. (04:46)Collaborate on designing and implementing integration flow to automate account creation in Rev IO after bookkeeper approval and improve commission data synchronization into HubSpot and QuotaPath. (11:40)Share sandbox access credentials for Rev IO and HubSpot with team members to facilitate integration testing and development. (24:53)Participate in follow-up meeting to discuss sales dashboard and reporting requirements with Sara and Ryan to define detailed actuals, targets, and trending reports. (28:36)Sara HinesRequest current compensation plan documents from Ryan upon his return and coordinate a walkthrough with Jeremy to identify opportunities for improvement. (06:14)Lead the gathering and detailing of sales dashboard and reporting requirements to create a more robust view including actuals versus targets and trending information. (28:31)Coordinate scheduling of follow-up calls, confirming availability of team members and communicating meeting times effectively. (29:30)Cameron TaggartDevelop Statement of Work (SOW) based on discovered integration needs and commission calculation flows; reach out with questions as they arise to ensure alignment. (28:10)Coordinate scheduling of the next meeting for Tuesday at 11 AM Central to accommodate team availability including Ryan post-spring break. (29:30)Facilitate integration design approach for automating billing and commission data flow across HubSpot, Rev IO, and QuickBooks with sign-offs and alerts to ensure process accountability. (12:40)Weston BakerInvestigate and design integration approaches leveraging webhooks and APIs for syncing billing and commission data, focusing on HubSpot as the source of truth for product pricing and line item changes. (19:00)Participate in test environments and integration design discussions, reviewing Rev IO billing use cases and limitations to support effective implementation. (24:40)Christina EdwardsProvide input on implementation process workflow, validate current ClickUp system functionality, and coordinate future reflection of implementation status in HubSpot for improved visibility. (08:22)

habits to improve adoption and decision-making.Baseline sales reporting focus to support training and adoption centered on standard reports like sales performance, pipeline, forecasting, and lead conversion dashboards (07:08)Jeremy Wiley emphasized the need to solidify basic reporting before adding specialized views.Cameron Taggart noted current underuse of existing dashboards and the need to clarify essential reports.Sarah Hines to provide input on sales dashboard preferences and additional best practices from her experience.The goal is to align dashboards with how sales reps understand and operate, improving usability and adoption.Tiered reporting templates to serve different roles include personalized views for reps, leadership, and executives to support performance tracking (11:42)Executive dashboards must show pipeline health, probability of close, and actuals versus targets in real time.Jeremy highlighted Salesforce’s limitations in accurate forecasting and how HubSpot could improve this with better calculation and visualization.Kyle Harbuck suggested leveraging HubSpot deal scoring and flagging features to track deal aging and activity patterns for pipeline hygiene.This layered reporting approach is designed to guide sales reps and leadership with relevant, actionable insights.Integration and data challenges impacting reporting accuracy focus on syncing sales data with accounting systems like QuickBooks to track revenue versus actuals reliably (32:30)Cameron raised concerns about whether QuickBooks integration is seamless or requires extra work to generate revenue reports.Jeremy stressed the importance of minimizing manual data entry and relying on system-generated data from quoting through invoicing to reduce errors.The team agreed tracking gaps between forecasted and actual revenue is critical for leadership’s decision-making.This integration is pivotal to achieving accurate, actionable KPIs in dashboards.Next steps include sharing detailed reports and screen prints for feedback to ensure reporting meets practical needs before finalizing system configuration (31:30)Jeremy plans to send a write-up of key reports and visual examples for review.Feedback will help identify unnecessary reports and clarify requirements.These deliverables will guide the proposal and implementation plan.The team aims to ensure no reporting gaps block sales process improvements.CRM Migration and Data NormalizationMoving to HubSpot requires careful data migration and cleaning with a focus on practical priorities and minimizing disruption.Prioritizing key data sets for migration to support operations quickly starts with customer lists, active pipeline, and leads, leaving inactive data archived (29:33)Kyle Harbuck recommended starting with the most complete and current data, then deciding what legacy info to archive.Jeremy wants to ensure operational systems like QuotaPath and accounting get synced early to solve urgent pain points.Cameron confirmed the importance of getting existing accounts into HubSpot to build a fresh system.This approach balances speed with data quality and usability.Establishing a cutoff date and normalization plan to avoid dual system complications and data inconsistencies during migration (28:56)Jeremy raised concerns about how far back to migrate and how to handle data overlap between Salesforce and HubSpot.Cameron noted discovery will uncover what data is essential and what can be left behind.The team expects to define clear ownership and timelines for finalizing migration scope.Normalizing data will include deciding on lead statuses, lifecycle stages, and parent-child account relationships.Leveraging HubSpot’s unique CRM features to fit the sales process involves deciding on lead objects, status workflows, and lifecycle stages that reflect real-world sales actions (20:00)Jeremy and Sarah will work closely to map how sales reps currently operate and what terminology and workflow make sense.Kyle emphasized aligning CRM processes with sales enablement to drive adoption and consistent usage.Playbooks and automation in HubSpot will guide reps through the sales stages and data entry.This alignment aims to reduce "wild west" usage and improve data accuracy.Managing dependencies with marketing and operations teams to ensure lead generation and account data flows smoothly into the new CRM without disrupting campaigns (22:36)Jeremy proposed involving Bridget and John from marketing to understand current lead gen systems and integrate them properly.The team agreed to leave marketing lead handling untouched unless major changes are needed, focusing first on sales data migration.This cross-team coordination will prevent silos and data gaps.The overall goal is to create a unified, operationally efficient CRM environment.Sales Process Definition and AdoptionClear sales stages, definitions, and process discipline are essential to consistent CRM use and reliable pipeline management.Current sales stage usage is inconsistent and needs standardization with simple, self-identifying definitions to simplify rep workflows (15:43)Jeremy is conducting a litmus test with veteran reps to validate and refine sales stage definitions.Cameron acknowledged the “wild west” state where reps use stages differently, causing reporting inaccuracies.The plan is to rename or eliminate confusing stages to improve clarity and adoption.Training and process management will enforce consistent use.Process alignment as the foundation for CRM success with clearly defined sales cycle steps and minimum criteria for stage progression (18:37)Kyle described process mapping as the "bible" for CRM setup and enablement, ensuring everyone follows the same steps.HubSpot’s Playbooks and automation will embed this process into daily sales activities.This discipline helps reps know exactly what to do at each stage and what qualifies a lead to progress.Alignment reduces deal stagnation and improves pipeline hygiene.Pipeline hygiene through deal scoring and activity monitoring to identify stalled deals and trigger appropriate actions like putting deals on hold or re-engaging (14:27)Kyle highlighted using HubSpot’s deal score and flags for inactivity duration and activity frequency.Jeremy emphasized the importance of understanding why deals stall and managing realistic pipeline expectations.Activity reports must convert call counts into meaningful pipeline movement.This approach aims to keep pipeline data accurate and actionable.Shadowing and coaching sales reps during meetings to observe and improve how HubSpot supports real-time sales conversations (16:40)Jeremy plans to shadow reps like Ryan to see firsthand how they use CRM in meetings.This on-the-ground insight will inform process tweaks and training priorities.Coaching will focus on making the CRM a natural part of sales conversations, not a burden.This effort supports long-term adoption and data integrity.Operational Efficiency and System IntegrationImproving operational workflows and system integrations is critical to reduce manual work and errors.Goal to eliminate manual rep outreach except for personal touches by automating operational communications and outreach templates (27:04)Jeremy wants reps only to manually contact clients to greet or thank them, with all other follow-ups automated.This requires mapping the entire sales lifecycle into system-driven touchpoints and Playbooks.Automation will increase consistency and reduce workload.This approach improves customer experience and rep efficiency.Integration of CPQ (Configure, Price, Quote) and QuotaPath into HubSpot to support deal configuration and commission reporting (24:16)Cameron highlighted the need to replicate CPQ functionality in HubSpot, ideally less manually than current processes.QuotaPath integration will enable transparent commission calculation and statements for reps.QuickBooks integration is also a priority to streamline accounting and invoicing workflows.These integrations will reduce errors and administrative overhead.Data normalization as a key operational challenge to ensure clean, consistent data flows across systems (28:18)Jeremy flagged the need to define how much historical data to migrate and how to handle data discrepancies.Cameron and Kyle agreed discovery will reveal what data is critical versus archival.The team expects to balance thoroughness with pragmatism to avoid overload.Clean data is foundational for efficient system operation and reliable reporting.Cross-team coordination to avoid disruption during migration by prioritizing sales data migration while keeping marketing lead processes stable (23:30)Jeremy noted marketing leads are currently managed mostly in HubSpot and Bridget is still ramping up.The team decided to keep marketing lead processes steady to focus on sales operations first.This phased approach reduces risk and complexity.Coordination ensures smooth transition and ongoing campaign effectiveness.Team Collaboration and Next StepsThe team emphasized continuous communication, shared understanding, and detailed planning to move forward efficiently.Commitment to open communication and no blockers with Jeremy offering support to prevent delays in the transition (35:26)Jeremy stated he does not want to be a stopping point and is ready to help remove obstacles.Cameron and Kyle agreed to maintain close coordination on scope and deliverables.This attitude supports agile progress and quick issue resolution.The team showed readiness to act promptly on feedback.Working sessions and training planned to deepen understanding and finalize CRM configuration (22:36)Jeremy requested focused sessions to be “Sherpa’d” through best practices and key system features.The team anticipates involving marketing and operations stakeholders as needed.These collaborative workshops aim to align all parties and clarify requirements.Training will help ensure the system fits real business processes.Proposal development based on clarified **2026-02-27T01:15:16.583Z — Sayer | HubSpot/revops**

Jeremy & Ryan,

Great talking through HubSpot with you yesterday. Apologies about missing
today, we didn't see invite until it was too late. But we have the
recording and everything we need to understand where we can help.

Based on everything you described, this is right in our wheelhouse. The
main areas where we can help are:

   - Salesforce → HubSpot migration: data mapping/cleanup + building the
   core CRM architecture
   - Quoting workflow: property/CSV-driven quoting, cleaner quote output,
   faster internal process
   - Commissions: visibility + reporting for reps/managers (and/or
   evaluating a better-fit tool)
   - Training + change management: get sales + CS confident so adoption
   sticks


Two quick questions so we don’t guess:

   - Where do you want to start first (CRM migration, quoting, commissions,
   billing, enablement)? I'd assume CRM but you tell us.
   - Is your preference to tackle this in one push, or phase it?


If it’s easier, happy to jump on a short call to help you all decide the
best starting point and sequence.

Thank you,

-- 

Cameron Taggart

Partner | Sayer

+1 972.746.9482 

1901 N. Akard St. Dallas, TX 75201

### Sayer | sales dashboards | HelloSpoke
*2026-04-14 16:10 UTC*

**Overview**

- **Sales Process Alignment:** Align sales processes with CRM to improve adoption and address inconsistent usage among reps.

- **CRM Migration Urgency:** Move data to HubSpot to resolve operational issues and ensure clean, normalized data during migration.

- **Phased Data Migration:** Start with inactive customers and current pipeline to minimize complexity and risk during migration.

- **Reporting Needs:** Create real-time executive dashboards that accurately reflect sales performance and forecast revenue gaps.

- **Lead Management Focus:** Define clear sales stages and link raw leads to qualified leads for ongoing nurturing and revenue growth.

- **Operational Efficiency:** Automate workflows to reduce manual tasks, allowing reps to focus on selling and improve reporting accuracy.

**Action Items**


**Jeremy Wiley**
Send detailed report and dashboard write-up including KPIs and screen prints to assist project scope and system setup (22:17)

### QuotaPath <> HelloSpoke: Data Scoping
*2026-04-08 17:00 UTC*

- **Data Integration**: Invoice data flows from Rev IO to QuickBooks Online and HubSpot for centralized tracking.

- **Commission Splits**: Handled at the product level; large commissions may be paid in installments with manual overrides.

- **Implementation Timeline**: Expected within 30 to 60 days, with preliminary pricing around $9,000 annually, 25% off list price.

- **Upcoming Discovery Workshop**: A deep dive session is scheduled to analyze current Salesforce usage and outline HubSpot setup and processes.

*2026-03-30 17:00 UTC*

**Overview**

- **Current Commission Process:** Manual, error-prone system causes over/underpayments; new methods needed for efficiency and transparency.

- **Sales Structure Overview:** Simple commission model; sales manager earns 2% override; reps have 10% and 20% commissions.

### HelloSpoke + HubSpot Growth
*2026-02-25 21:00 UTC*

**Overview**

- **Adoption Focus:** Emphasizing user adoption over implementation to drive efficiency and scale; training ensures effective system use.

- **HubSpot Advantages:** HubSpot is easier to customize and manage internally, reducing reliance on costly external consultants compared to Salesforce.

- **Cost Management:** Sayer offers flexible support to minimize surprise fees, addressing concerns about expensive change requests with Salesforce.

- **Simplifying Sales Workflows:** Team aims to reduce complexity by eliminating duplicate accounts and standardizing sales stages for better accuracy.

- **Automation Goals:** Automating follow-up tasks and email tracking is essential for streamlining sales processes while maintaining necessary human interactions.

- **Implementation Collaboration:** Clear roles and internal coordination, led by a project champion, ensure smooth CRM transition and execution.
